text:
Clouds play a critical role in reshaping the composition of atmospheric aerosols by acting as a reactive medium where gas- and particulate-phase substances interact. To explore the molecular makeup of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) and the effects of cloud processing, we collected two samples of supercooled cloud water at the Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.) near Steamboat Springs, Colorado. Using reversed-phase solid-phase extraction and ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, we identified approximately 3,000 distinct molecular formulas in each sample. 

The detected species spanned molecular weights up to 700 Da and fell into several elemental categories: oxygen-only (CHO), nitrogen-containing (CHNO), and sulfur-containing (CHOS and CHNOS) compounds. The average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio was approximately 0.6, suggesting a more oxidized state than typically found in water-soluble organic carbon from aerosol studies—a shift likely driven by aqueous-phase oxidation within the clouds. 

Our analysis reveals that the AOM is heavily influenced by biogenic secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and residential wood combustion. Notably, 60% of the CHO formulas in the cloud water were identical to those found in laboratory SOA generated from the ozonolysis of monoterpenes like α-pinene and d-limonene. CHNO compounds were the most abundant and frequent species, linked to NOx-influenced oxidation and wood smoke. Furthermore, the nighttime sample showed a higher prevalence of multi-nitrogen formulas, pointing to the importance of nitrate radical chemistry in the dark. We also identified several sulfur species containing reduced sulfur, though their specific origins require further study. These findings provide an unambiguous molecular fingerprint of cloud water organic anions in the Rocky Mountains, offering a clearer understanding of how aqueous chemistry modifies the atmosphere.
